1: Job's counter-speech opens without the conventional wisdom-claims Eliphaz deployed. The stark speech-introduction marks the tonal shift: Job does not argue epistemically but existentially.
2: Mĕnaḥămê ʿāmāl: 'miserable comforters' — ʿāmāl (trouble/toil) qualifies the noun: the comforters produce the very trouble they claim to address. A devastating reversal of the comforter role. Kullĕkem (all of you): Job indicts the entire group.
3: Dibrê rûaḥ: 'words of wind/spirit' — empty talk (cf. Job 15:2). Mah-yammîṣĕkā: 'what emboldens you?' — from ʾmṣ (to be strong, courageous): what gives you the courage to keep answering? Job questions the source of their confidence.
4: Gam-ʾānōkî kĕṣbkem ʾădabbērāh: 'I too could speak as you do' — Job's hypothetical: if the roles were reversed, he could pile up millîm (words) against them. ʾānîʿāh ʿălêkem bĕrōʾšî: 'shake my head at you' — the gesture of contempt (cf. Psalms 22:8; Lamentations 2:15).
5: ʾĂḥazbēq bĕpî: 'I would strengthen you with my mouth' — the conditional opposite of what the friends have done. Job claims he would have offered genuine nîḥûm (comfort) rather than accusation, had the situation been reversed. The contrast exposes the friends' failure of pastoral care.
6: ʾim-ʾădabbĕrāh lōʾ-yēḥāśēk kĕʾēbî: 'if I speak, the pain is not held back' — kĕʾēb (pain, anguish) persists regardless of whether Job speaks or stays silent. The dilemma of the sufferer: neither speech nor silence provides relief. ʾeḥĕšāh (I am silent) — used of God's silence in Psalms 50:3, here of the sufferer's exhaustion.
7: ʿAttāh hĕlaʾānî: 'But now he has utterly exhausted me' — the shift to second person addressing God directly. Lʾh (to be weary, exhausted) in Hiphil: God has worn Job out. Hăšimmôtāh (made desolate): the congregation/circle of Job's community is now made empty.
8: Wattiqmĕṭēnî: 'you have wasted/shriveled me' — from qmṭ (to wrinkle, shrivel — hapax in this sense). Kāḥaš (emaciation, failure) is the unusual term for what Job's physical condition has become: his shrunken flesh is itself a witness (ʿēḏ) against him in the court of public perception.
9: ʾappô ṭārap: 'his wrath has torn me' — the verb ṭārap (to tear prey) is the predator's act. Yiḥărōq-ʿālay bĕšinnāyw: 'gnashes his teeth at me' — divine hostility figured as predatory animal. Ṣārî (my enemy/adversary): Job attributes the attacks to God-as-enemy.
10: Pāṣû ʿālay bĕpêhem: 'they open wide their mouths at me' — the communal mockery gesture (cf. Psalms 22:14; Lamentations 2:16). Yakkûnî bĕleḥî: 'they strike my cheek' — judicial humiliation, a physical act of contempt. Yitḥabbrû ʿālay: they gather as a band against me.
11: Yasgîrēnî ʾēl: 'God has surrendered me' — the term sāgar (to shut up, deliver over) is the treaty/covenant term for handing someone over to an enemy (cf. Obad 14; Psalms 31:9). Job charges God with delivering him over to ʾăwîl (the evildoer) and rĕšāʿîm (the wicked).
12: Šālēw hāyîtî: 'I was living in ease' — from šlw (to be at ease, prosperous) — the condition before the disruption. Yĕparpĕrēnî (shattered me): intensive Pilpel form for repeated shattering. The sequence of violent verbs (grabbed, broke, set up as target) creates a courtroom of divine violence.
13: Rabbāyw (his archers): God's multiple marksmen. Yipĕlaḥ kilĕyôtāy: 'he slashes into my kidneys' — kilāyāh (kidneys) as the seat of emotional life, the innermost self. Yišpôk lāʾārṣ mĕrērātî: 'spills my gall on the ground' — mĕrērāh (gall/bile) = bitterness, the substance of suffering poured out.
14: Yipĕrĕṣēnî pereṣ ʿal-pĕnê-pāreṣ: 'breach after breach' — the cognate construction pereṣ/pāraṣ (breach/to breach) intensifies the destruction. Gibbôr (mighty warrior): God depicted as a great warrior charging the breach he himself opened. The warrior-God motif is used here paradoxically against the covenant people's representative.
15: Śaq tāpartî ʿălê giltî: 'I have sewn sackcloth over my skin' — sackcloth as mourning garment, here literally sewn onto the body. ʿôlaltî lĕʿāpār qarnî: 'I have pressed my dignity down into the dust' — qeren (horn) = dignity, honor, power; pressed into the dust = total humiliation.
16: Pānay ḥāmarmĕrāh mibbekî: 'my face is flushed from weeping' — ḥāmar (to boil, foam red) describes the inflamed skin. ʿal-ʿapʿappay ṣalmāwet: 'on my eyelids is the shadow of death' — ṣalmāwet (deep shadow/shadow of death): either a compound of ṣēl + māwet or an intensive form for 'deep darkness'; the death-shadow rests on his very eyelids.
17: ʿal lōʾ-ḥāmās bĕkappāyw: 'though no violence has been done by his hands' — ḥāmās (violence, injustice) is the comprehensive term for covenant-breaking violence (Genesis 6:11; Habakkuk 1:2). Job asserts his innocence using the highest-stakes vocabulary. Tĕhillātî (my prayer) is pure — liturgical and moral integrity maintained.
18: ʾErṣ ʾal-tĕkassî dāmî: 'O earth, do not cover my blood' — the appeal to the earth to preserve the blood-witness, drawing on the Abel-precedent (Genesis 4:10: qôl dĕmê ʾāḥîkā ṣōʿăqîm). Zĕʿāqātî (my cry) is the ṣĕʿāqāh — the oppressed person's cry that reaches God. Job demands his cry remain audible, refusing burial.
19: ʿĒḏî baššāmayim: 'my witness is in heaven' — the ʿēḏ (witness/testifier) is a heavenly figure parallel to śāhĕḏî (my advocate). The latter term śāhēḏ is an Aramaic loanword (cf. Aram. sāheḏ) appearing only here; it means 'one who testifies in defense.' These two parallel terms (witness + advocate) describe a heavenly being who holds Job's case. This is the same figure anticipated in Job 9:33 (mediator) and named in Job 19:25 (Redeemer).
20: Mĕlîṣay rēʿāy: 'my friends are my scorners' — lîṣ (to mock, scorn) — the friends who should comfort now mock. ʾEl-ʾĕlôah: yet Job's eye (ʿênî) pours out tears to God. The contrast between human rejection and divine address is the structural center of Job's faith.
21: Wĕyôkaḥ lĕgeber ʿim-ʾĕlôah: 'if only a mortal man could argue/plead with God' — yākaḥ (to reason together, argue, adjudicate): the legal-wisdom term for formal disputation. Geber (mortal man) vs. ʾĕlôah (God): the ontological gap that makes the lawsuit impossible without a mediator. Bēn ʾîš lĕrēʿēhû: 'as between a man and his neighbor' — Job longs for human-level accessibility to divine adjudication.
22: Šĕnôt mispar: 'the few numbered years' — the appointed span, small. ʾOrāḥ lōʾ-ʾāšûb: 'a road from which there is no return' — the irreversibility of death that makes Job's urgency understandable. The lawsuit must be settled before the one-way road is taken.
